title: Tamil Meen Kuzhambu (Fish Curry)

ingredients.name: seer fish (vanjaram) or any firm fish, cut into steaks

subs.name: tiger prawns — add at the same stage but reduce cook time to 5 minutes
2 tbsp tamarind paste diluted in 250 ml water
any neutral cooking oil
subs.notes: gingelly oil gives the Tamil-coastal character

steps.text: Soak the tamarind in 300 ml warm water for 10 minutes. Squeeze and strain to extract the juice; discard the pulp and seeds. Set the tamarind water aside.
Heat gingelly oil in a heavy kadai over medium heat. Splutter the mustard seeds, then add fenugreek seeds and fry for 15 seconds — do not let the fenugreek brown or it will turn bitter.
Add the curry leaves and sliced shallots. Fry until the shallots turn golden and translucent, about 5 minutes, then add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 2 more minutes until the raw smell is gone.
Add the tomatoes with a pinch of salt and cook until they fully collapse, releasing their juices and beginning to dry out, about 5 minutes.
Lower the flame and add red chilli powder, coriander powder and turmeric. Stir for 30 seconds, then pour in the tamarind water. Bring to a boil on high heat.

Reduce to a gentle simmer. Slide in the fish steaks carefully and cook uncovered for 10 minutes, gently shaking the pan — do not stir or the fish will break apart. The gravy should thicken slightly and the fish will turn opaque throughout.

Taste the gravy: it should be balanced between sour, spicy and salty. Adjust seasoning, scatter coriander leaves and serve. The kuzhambu tastes even better the next day after the fish has soaked up the masala.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: fish → tiger prawns — add at the same stage but reduce cook time to 5 minutes (changes to shellfish allergen); tamarind → 2 tbsp tamarind paste diluted in 250 ml water; gingelly-oil → any neutral cooking oil (gingelly oil gives the Tamil-coastal character); shallot → 1 large onion, sliced
